title: The Container and the Content
meta: Option 3 Submission | Bilal Ghalib | CMC PGCert | March 2026

paragraphs.text: Bilal Ghalib | CMC PGCert Islam & Psychology | Module 2, Option 3 | March 2026
The critique is not of Western culture as such, but of a specific ontological inheritance: a post-Augustinian suspicion of creation, body, and the senses that the Islamic tradition never shared.
paragraphs.why: Prevents the essay being read as anti-Western polemic. Names the specific genealogy.

paragraphs.text: Can Islamic psychology be built by integrating Islamic content into Western psychological categories, or does it require a paradigm grounded in Islamic epistemology and ontology? This essay argues for the latter, and for a specific reason: the categories themselves are not neutral containers waiting to be filled. They encode an anthropology, the buffered, autonomous self, that is historically contingent, philosophically particular, and structurally incompatible with the Islamic account of the human being. Integration approaches inherit this anthropology even as they substitute Islamic content. The result is a psychology that speaks Arabic but thinks in English. An epistemology-first paradigm avoids this error because it builds from the tradition's own account of what the human is, what knowledge is for, and what it means for things to go well.

The reason the history of subjectivity matters is that modern psychology is not a neutral science of a timeless human subject; it is a discipline shaped by, and partly designed for, the buffered self. The argument proceeds in four parts. First, it traces the modern transformation of subjectivity that produced this self. Second, it examines how two Western schools, psychoanalysis and analytical psychology, absorbed and sealed its assumptions about religion. Third, it evaluates integration, indigenisation, and epistemology-first approaches within modern Islamic psychology. Fourth, it synthesises these strands and draws out implications. The comparative judgement sustained throughout is that epistemology-first provides a more robust basis than integration or indigenisation because the latter inherit the anthropological and epistemic assumptions of modern psychology even when they import Islamic content.

The self that modern psychology studies did not always exist. Charles Taylor (2007) traces a five-century transformation from the 'porous self,' open to transcendent meaning and embedded in a cosmos charged with spiritual significance, to the 'buffered self,' sealed off from transcendence and oriented toward internal states. The mechanism matters because it was not a story of subtraction but of transformation, driven paradoxically by religious reform itself.

The porous self does not draw a sharp boundary between inner and outer, psychological and spiritual. God was not a hypothesis to accept or reject but the medium in which everything moved. The classical Islamic philosophical tradition operated within a comparable ecology of knowledge. Philosophers like al-Kindi (d. 873), al-Farabi (d. 950), and Ibn Sina (d. 1037) engaged deeply with Aristotelian thought, but within a civilisational context that maintained living chains of transmission connecting the knower to communities of worship, legal reasoning, and spiritual formation. The knowledge had a home. When Islamic philosophical conclusions arrived in medieval Europe through Latin translations, what crossed was the product: the rational arguments, the logical methods, the cosmological schemas. What did not cross was the revelatory epistemology, the *adab* (ethics of intellectual conduct), the *isnad* (chain of accountability) within which those thinkers had operated. The severance of product from process would shape everything that followed.

The Reformation intensified religion and in doing so began the sealing. By insisting that every individual must have a direct relationship with God, without priestly mediation or sacramental magic, it moved the centre of spiritual gravity inward. This was deeply pious. But it created a self-examining, disciplined individual whose faith was interior rather than cosmic. The next century discovered that the discipline worked even without the devotion: the Protestant work ethic continued to run after the Protestant part was removed. God was relegated to providential Deism, a clockmaker who wound the universe and stepped back. By the eighteenth century, Enlightenment science had reduced a cosmos filled with God's grandeur to an impersonal mechanism. Taylor's crucial insight is that this occurred not because science disproved God but because the already-buffered self found a mechanistic universe intuitive. The causation runs from selfhood to worldview, not the reverse. The result is what Taylor calls the 'immanent frame': a world that explains itself without reference to anything beyond itself. As Abdal Hakim Murad observes: 'Once we said *know thyself*. Nowadays we say *show thyself*' (Murad, 2025). Expressive individualism demands that the self perform without knowing what it is performing.

Nietzsche (1886/1998) named what happens inside this frame once inherited transcendence loses authority. If God is dead, and Nietzsche's point was that Christian civilisation killed him through its own commitment to truth, then all values must be created, not received. Once limits are no longer received as part of the structure of reality, they must be self-imposed, strategically negotiated, or externally enforced, which is why modernity oscillates between fantasies of inward limitlessness and outward projects of mastery. Nietzsche himself understood this required a counter-constraint: eternal recurrence, the demand that one must will each act as if it would repeat forever. But his successors discarded the constraint while keeping the project, producing a culture in which the self is conceived as both the source of value and the object of its own optimisation.

Philip Rieff (1966) diagnosed the cultural consequence. Where once culture operated through interdicts, through patterns of 'thou shalt nots' that gave people the means of 'controlling the infinite variety of panic and emptiness to which they are disposed,' the therapeutic age 'demands less and permits more.' 'Religious man was born to be saved; psychological man is born to be pleased.' Well-being, once a by-product of right orientation, became the primary goal. Rieff identified a further structural problem: drawing on Sullivan, he observed that sublimation works only unconsciously, and that making the mechanism explicit disables the transformation. This is directly relevant to integration: converting *dhikr* into a 'mindfulness technique' is not translation but dissolution of the practice into a category that cannot hold it.
The condition is not merely passive inheritance. It is active buffering: the self that wants to feel good at all costs, even against its own morality, closing its own eyes.
paragraphs.notes: Shifts from passive state (inherited) to active choice (chosen). More Islamic: kufr is a verb.
paragraphs.why: Reframes the buffered self from something you inherit to something you do. Kufr is active covering.

paragraphs.text: The directionality of the self shifted. The porous self receives: meaning descends, knowledge arrives as gift, the scholar is shaped by what flows into it from beyond. The buffered self projects: meaning is constructed, knowledge is produced, the human being acts outward upon a world stripped of intrinsic significance. The Islamic tradition names these orientations: *'ubudiyyah* (عبودية, servitude, the self constituted by its relation to the Real) and *istighna'* (استغناء, self-sufficiency, the self sealed off from what it needs). The buffered self is, in Islamic terms, structurally incapable of receiving what it most needs because it has no category for reception.

The Quranic term for disbelief, kufr, literally means to cover. The buffered self covers submission with rationalization and explanation. Taylor described the operation in sociological language. The Quran named it first.
paragraphs.notes: Kufr = buffered self. Structural identity, not metaphor.
paragraphs.why: Connects Quranic terminology to Taylor's concept. The tradition named this before sociology did.

paragraphs.text: The difference is visible in a shared thought experiment. Ibn Sina's 'flying man' (c. 1027) and Descartes' *cogito* (1641) both strip the self of sensory input and ask what remains. But the containers differ. Ibn Sina discovers a *nafs* (نفس), a soul already oriented toward God. When stuck on a philosophical problem, he prayed, not as superstition but because his psychology held that the purified imaginative faculty (*al-mutakhayyila*) can receive intelligible forms from the Active Intellect. Prayer opens the channel. Knowledge, in this paradigm, is partly receptive: it flows toward the prepared soul from a source beyond it. Descartes discovers a thinking substance and rebuilds from his own certainty alone. Knowledge, in his paradigm, is entirely produced. Same experiment. Different container. Ibn Sina's porosity produced the polymath who could work across twenty-seven fields because all knowledge flows from a single Source. Descartes' buffering produced the specialist sealed within one method.

Psychoanalysis, as Freud developed it, received the buffered self and built a psychology for it. His treatment of religion enacts a disciplinary inversion: religion is placed under psychology's explanatory jurisdiction, and revelation becomes data to be interpreted by a theory of the psyche rather than a source of knowledge capable of judging psychology itself. In *The Future of an Illusion* (1927), Freud described religion as 'the universal obsessional neurosis of humanity,' a wish-fulfilment structure born from infantile helplessness. What the tradition calls *wahy* (وحي, revelation), Freud's system can only code as the unconscious breaching its containment. The problem is not that Freud was entirely wrong about psychological projection: projection exists. The problem is that psychoanalysis has no ontological space for the possibility that some of what flows toward the self from beyond is real. The container has already decided.

This matters because Freud's anthropological assumptions seeped beyond psychoanalysis into the therapeutic culture Rieff diagnosed: the background assumption that religious experience is 'really' psychological, that transcendence is 'really' projection, that the sacred is 'really' the unconscious dressed up.

Analytical psychology, as Jung developed it, offers a more sympathetic alternative, and the contrast is instructive. His concept of the collective unconscious restored a shared layer of psychic life deeper than individual experience; his archetypes gave religious symbols genuine psychological significance. Jung famously stated that among his patients over thirty-five, there was not one whose problem was not ultimately that of finding a religious outlook on life. Yet analytical psychology's hospitality to religion operates within the same container. Revelation becomes archetype. The divine becomes a psychic structure. Prayer becomes active imagination. God, in Jungian psychology, is 'real' as a psychological reality, not as an ontological one. The *qalb* (قلب) that the Islamic tradition describes as an organ of spiritual perception, capable of genuine contact with the Real, is, in Jung's framework, the ego encountering its own depths. Even the most religion-friendly Western school retains the buffered self's fundamental assumption: sovereignty over the sacred. The container spiritualises. It does not open. Sotillos (2025) names this precisely: the 'deification of the psyche,' in which the sacred is welcomed only after being relocated inside the psyche as archetype rather than recognised as ontological reality. Mahmoud (2025) recognises Jung's project as 'a genuine response to the rise of secularism,' yet one that ultimately 'repudiate[s] the metaphysical' (Jung, 1929): the container remains sealed even as it opens a window painted to look like the sky.

Al-Ghazali (d. 1111) diagnosed a related error centuries earlier. In the *Munqidh min al-Dalal*, he warned that the precision of mathematical proof seduces people into thinking that philosophical speculation carries the same certainty, and then they reject revelation because philosophy cannot prove it. The error is not inquiry but category confusion: applying the method of one domain to the questions of another, and declaring the questions invalid when the method fails. Ibn Taymiyyah (d. 1328) offered the constructive resolution: his principle *sarih al-ma'qul muwafiq li-sahih al-manqul* ('sound reason always accords with authentic revelation') asserts that when they appear to conflict, the error lies in either the reasoning or the interpretation, never in the sources themselves, because the Source is one (El-Tobgui, 2020, pp. 132-143). This is the framework Europe never received.

Seligman's PERMA model (2011) is the most developed expression of this architecture applied to flourishing. 'Meaning' in PERMA is whatever the individual experiences as meaningful. Seligman defines it as 'belonging to and serving something you believe is bigger than the self' (2011, p. 17). The operative word is *believe*. Not 'something that *is* bigger.' Within positive psychology itself, van Zyl et al. (2023) identify recurring critiques: decontextualised neoliberal ideology, lack of proper theorising, and commercialised 'happiness' ventures. Health replaces goodness. The framework has no category for the difference.

The question of Islamic psychology's foundations has been contested since Malik Badri's *The Dilemma of Muslim Psychologists* (1979), which challenged the uncritical adoption of Western psychological frameworks among Muslim scholars and practitioners. Badri diagnosed the problem and called for critical filtering, but did not himself deliver the paradigmatic alternative that later scholars would develop. Haque et al. (2016) map three levels of response: culturally sensitive delivery, integration of Islamic concepts within Western frameworks, and Islamic psychology grounded in Islamic epistemology. Most current work operates at the first two levels.

These levels are worth distinguishing. *Integration* takes Western categories as given and fills them with Islamic content: mapping *shukr* onto PERMA's positive emotion, using Quranic verses alongside CBT protocols. *Indigenisation* adapts psychology to Muslim cultural contexts, adjusting language, norms, and therapeutic settings, but may leave the underlying ontology untouched: the self remains the buffered individual, assessed by self-report, understood through Western developmental stages. Both are valuable at the clinical level. Neither addresses the paradigmatic question. The *epistemology-first* approach, by contrast, begins from Islamic anthropology and sources: not adapting Western categories to Muslim settings but constructing categories from the tradition's own account of the human being. A fourth approach, the Islamization of Knowledge movement (al-Faruqi, 1982), sought to reconstruct entire disciplines from Islamic first principles, though its institutional focus on curricula differs from the anthropological ground later scholars articulate. This essay argues that only the epistemology-first approach avoids the container error, because it does not inherit the architecture of the buffered self.

The limitation of integration is structural. Islamic concepts, *shukr*, *ihsan*, *ukhuwwah*, *tawakkul*, *istiqama*, can be mapped onto PERMA's five pillars, as Aycan (2022) demonstrates. The Islamic content is genuine. But the receiving structure has already decided that well-being consists of five measurable pillars running in parallel. What cannot be expressed within this structure is the Islamic tradition's central claim: that these are not independent domains but expressions of a single orientation. *'Ubudiyyah* (عبودية), servitude to God, is the ground from which *shukr*, *ihsan*, *ukhuwwah*, *tawakkul*, and *istiqama* arise as consequences, not as components to be optimised individually. Map them onto PERMA and you lose the root. Gratitude becomes a technique for mood regulation. Trust becomes a coping mechanism. Remembrance becomes mindfulness. The words survive. The orientation is lost. The Quran captures the consequence: *wa idha qila lahum la tufsidu fi al-ard qalu innama nahnu muslihun, ala innahum hum al-mufsiduna wa lakin la yash'urun* ('When it is said to them, "Do not cause corruption on the earth," they say, "We are only reformers!" Unquestionably, it is they who are the corrupters, but they perceive it not,' Q 2:11-12). Their PERMA meaning score would be excellent. Their orientation is catastrophic. As Mahmoud (2025) argues, the mandate of Islamic psychology should be understood against a broader backdrop than the pathologies of the modern condition: the field should not be developed 'as just a response and a reaction all the time.' Integration is reactive by design. Epistemology-first is constructive.

The epistemology-first approach begins from different ground. Rothman and Coyle (2018) built a model of the human soul from Islamic sources through grounded theory analysis with eighteen scholars and clinicians. *Fitrah* (فطرة) is the innate disposition toward God, truth, and goodness: what the human is and what the human is for are the same thing. *Qalb* (قلب) is the heart as organ of spiritual perception; *'aql* (عقل) is a function of the *qalb*, not autonomous. *Nafs* (نفس) has developmental stages: *al-nafs al-ammara bi'l-su'* (النفس الأمارة بالسوء, 'the soul that commands to evil,' Q 12:53), *al-nafs al-lawwama* (النفس اللوامة, 'the self-reproaching soul,' Q 75:2), *al-nafs al-mutma'inna* (النفس المطمئنة, 'the soul at peace,' Q 89:27-28). *Ruh* (روح) is the spirit connecting human to divine, a component with no parallel in secular psychology because secular psychology has no ontological space for it.

This is not a list but a system with directional logic. The *ruh* orients the *qalb*, which governs the *'aql*, which disciplines the *nafs*. In Maslow's hierarchy, each level can be addressed independently; the levels are stacked but separable. In the Islamic model, the components are interdependent: disrupt any link and the whole system is affected. A heart veiled by heedlessness produces reasoning that is internally coherent but ultimately misguided. This is why the tradition insists that knowledge and practice cannot be separated: sound reasoning requires a sound heart, and a sound heart requires the daily disciplines of worship, remembrance, and moral accountability. The *'alim* who does not pray is not simply a hypocrite; he is an epistemically compromised knower.

The TIIP framework (Keshavarzi and Khan, 2020) provides the epistemological architecture: *wahy* (وحي, revelation), *istidlali* (استدلالي, rational-empirical investigation), and *hawass salima* (حواس سليمة, sound sense perception). Revelation sets the frame; reason and sense-data operate within it. Badri's selectivity principle remains: Western tools can be received without their anthropological baggage, provided the receiving framework is Islamic ontology. A breathing technique received by PERMA's anthropology becomes a tool for subjective calm. The same technique received as preparation for *dhikr* becomes a station in the movement of the *nafs* toward *mutma'inna*. The tool is the same. The receiving framework determines what it becomes (Rothman, 2022). Al-Kindi's *On Dispelling Sorrow* illustrates this from within the tradition itself: his cognitive-behavioural interventions resemble modern CBT, but operate within an anthropology where sorrow is attachment to the impermanent and the remedy is remembrance of divine sovereignty, not technique alone. Rothman (2025) makes the point concretely about Carl Rogers: the Rogerian method is essentially *muhasaba* (self-reckoning), 'but it's being with what is, it's just not being with Allah. That piece is missing.'

The difference is experiential. In salah, the movement is inward, toward something: you witness. In the gratitude journal, you pull from inside yourself, externalizing onto the page. You are the source. Same word, opposite architecture.
paragraphs.notes: Bilal's own practice as evidence. Receiving (salah) vs producing (journal).
paragraphs.why: THE concrete example of the container difference. From Bilal's own practice.

paragraphs.text: This leads to a distinction that integration approaches cannot generate from within their own categories. Therapeutic *'afiya* (عافية) is the absence of distress and the presence of positive functioning: what PERMA measures. It is real but fragile, dependent on circumstances. Constitutive *'afiya* is the soundness of the soul's orientation toward God: not a subjective feeling but a relational reality. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ wept when his son Ibrahim died. His eyes shed tears, his heart was grieved. But he said nothing that displeased Allah. That is constitutive *'afiya* in the midst of therapeutic distress.

Epistemology-first provides a more robust basis than integration or indigenisation because it does not inherit the container of the buffered self. The evidence for this claim extends beyond clinical settings: the same container that misreads a person also misreads a civilisation.

Eric Chaney (2023) uses three datasets containing over 42,000 authors to measure the proportion working on scientific topics (defined narrowly as medicine and mathematics, or broadly including astronomy and optics). The proportion declines. He is careful to blame 'institutions empowering religious elites, not Islam itself' (p. 6), and concedes that time-varying selection in Western scholarship 'could generate a spurious downward trend' (p. 16). His methodological care makes the underlying classification problem more, not less, visible. A reanalysis using 85,043 author records from the Virtual International Authority File complicates his finding: total authors quintuple from 4,333 to 23,236. The 'scientist' proportion drops because the denominator explodes with scholars in legal theory, theology, ethics, and spiritual formation. Adjusted for population (which declined through Mongol invasions and the Black Death), scientists per million *rose* from 0.82 to 1.44. The binary classification erases polymathy: 169 scholars work across both natural and Islamic sciences, because the boundary did not exist in their training.

El-Rouayheb (2008) confirms the pattern: even Birgevi (d. 1573), the inspiration for the puritanical Kadizadeli movement, classified mathematics and logic as *fard kifaya* (فرض كفاية, communal religious obligations). Ottoman colleges teaching the philosophical sciences may have doubled between 1650 and 1705. The commentary tradition (*sharh*) was not stagnation but a multi-generational knowledge architecture. Ibn Ata'illah al-Iskandari's *Hikam* (1309) generated commentaries by Ibn Abbad al-Rundi (1390), multiple commentaries by Ahmad Zarruq (1493), a major super-commentary by Ahmad ibn Ajiba (1809), a three-volume modern commentary by Sa'id Ramadan al-Bouti (taught in Damascus from 1974), and a published commentary by the living Shadhili shaykh Nuh Ha Mim Keller. The author of this essay studied the *Hikam* at Cambridge Muslim College with Dr Yusuf Jha: the text is still being transmitted, studied, and engaged with 700 years after it was written. Saliba (2007) notes that such commentaries were 'in some ways superior to' modern periodicals in their demand for comprehensive synthesis. Hirschler (2012) documents that the written word significantly expanded in 11th-15th century Egypt and Syria, with artisans attending scholarly reading sessions in Damascus. Dallal (2010) argues that the late decline was largely due to extrinsic factors, not structural intellectual deficiency. Keshavarzi (2025) documents a further erasure: the 1923 Latin script change in Turkey rendered an entire population illiterate vis-a-vis their own intellectual heritage overnight, severing access to at least 88 untranslated Ottoman psychology works. What Chaney codes as decline was the democratisation of an integrated knowledge ecology.

This is the container error applied to civilisational analysis. Chaney 'integrated' Islamic intellectual history into Western categories (science/not-science) and produced a finding (decline) that the categories themselves generated. PERMA 'integrates' Islamic spiritual concepts into Western categories (five parallel pillars) and produces a framework that has already excluded the root (*'ubudiyyah*) before the content arrives. In both cases, the container is the problem, not the content.

My own research on post-traumatic growth among Lebanese social entrepreneurs after the Beirut Port Blast (Ghalib, 2021) found that the character strengths most strongly predicting growth were humour, judgement, teamwork, and hope: strengths that are relational and virtue-oriented. They map more naturally onto an *'ubudiyyah* anthropology, humour as trust in providence, judgement as discernment (*firasa*), teamwork as solidarity (*ta'awun*), hope as reliance (*tawakkul*), than onto an autonomy framework. The data does not prove the Islamic model. But it is more intelligible within it.

Integration approaches inherit the container of the buffered self: the self-report scale, the individual as unit, flourishing as subjective state, knowledge as separable domains. They fill this container with genuine Islamic content, but the architecture determines what that content becomes. Epistemology-first provides a more robust basis because it does not offer better content for the same categories. It offers different categories, derived from a different account of the human subject.

The epistemology-first approach builds a different container. The self is *nafs* in developmental movement. Knowledge is *amana* (أمانة, trust) carried with *adab* (أدب, propriety). Flourishing is constitutive *'afiya*. The disciplines of the tradition, *salah*, *siyam*, *dhikr*, *mujahada*, are not wellness techniques but formative technologies that shape the self rather than serve it. You are not the customer. You are the material.

Three implications follow for the future development of Islamic psychology. Academically, the field must articulate its categories from its own sources, the work Rothman and Coyle (2018) and Keshavarzi and Khan (2020) have begun, rather than mapping onto Western architecture. This does not mean rejecting Western tools; Badri's selectivity principle stands. It means ensuring that the receiving framework is Islamic ontology, not the buffered self's account of itself. Communally, the tradition's institutional ecology offers a model: the madrasa producing integrated knowers, the *waqf* (وقف) funding scholars to serve communities, the *ijaza* (إجازة) ensuring accountability through living chains of transmission. Ethically, knowledge carried as *amana* reorients the purpose of inquiry: not 'what can we discover?' but 'what does creation need from us?'

The *hadith* captures the orientation: 'If the Hour comes and one of you has a sapling in his hand, let him plant it.' A civilisation that maintained this orientation through the destruction of cities, producing more scholars per capita in its darkest century than in its brightest, was not declining. It was operating from a different container. The task of Islamic psychology is not to integrate Islamic content into the buffered self's categories, but to build from the ground the tradition actually stands on.

The distinction is between a constitution, what something is made of, and a revelation, what something is called toward. Integration generates an ethic from the self's own constitution. Epistemology-first receives one.
paragraphs.notes: Constitution = self-defined. Revelation = given. The thesis compressed to one word-pair.
paragraphs.why: The essay's argument in its most compressed form.
